title: Assessing global urban CO2 removal

data_scope.study_type: literature assessment with quantitative synthesis (no new primary dataset)
data_scope.reviewed_item_count: not stated as a headline figure in main text

core_conclusion: Cities can act as carbon sinks rather than only sources. Synthesizing the literature, the authors quantify the global potential of four urban CDR options (carbon storage in vegetation, soils and buildings, plus indoor direct air capture) and find a substantial possible contribution to climate mitigation with human and environmental co-benefits, but deployment is constrained by uncertainties, economic barriers and governance/accounting gaps.
why_it_matters: Reframes the city from carbon source to potential sink; unifies four scattered CDR options into one comparable global assessment by 2050; highlights co-benefits beyond carbon; and honestly catalogues the uncertainties, costs and governance barriers that stall deployment.
why_nature: Global, transferable synthesis of urban CDR potential; couples climate, infrastructure and well-being; strongly policy-translatable for net-zero target-setting; and explicitly sets a forward-looking research agenda.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially explicit satellite-driven CDR mapping -> multi-source data fusion supply layer
Explainable ML on the barrier taxonomy -> ranked drivers of realized urban mitigation
Vegetation CDR recast as 2SFCA/R2SFCA green-infrastructure accessibility and equity
Multi-objective CDR siting -> transfer of my efficiency-equity EV optimization machinery
Conservation-constrained physics-informed graph for cross-sectoral carbon accounting
